era_context: The Syrian War (192–188 BCE) is in full swing. Rome, under the command of Manius Acilius Glabrio, is actively campaigning against Antiochus III in Greece. The Seleucid King's invasion of Greece has been checked, and Roman military superiority is becoming evident. The Ptolemaic Kingdom remains neutral but watches the conflict with concern. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen continues its period of stability. This is a year of major military conflict in the Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Battle of Thermopylae: Roman victory over Antiochus III
events.description: Roman forces under Manius Acilius Glabrio defeat Antiochus III's army at Thermopylae in central Greece. This battle is a decisive Roman victory and marks a turning point in the Syrian War. Antiochus III's invasion of Greece is effectively ended, and he is forced to retreat to Asia Minor. The battle demonstrates Roman military superiority and marks the beginning of Rome's dominance over the Hellenistic world. The victory is facilitated by a flanking maneuver that recalls the famous Persian strategy at the same location nearly 300 years earlier.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Thermopylae in 191 BCE is well-documented in multiple ancient sources. The date and outcome are firmly established.

events.title: Roman forces pursue Antiochus III into Asia Minor
events.description: Following the victory at Thermopylae, Roman forces pursue Antiochus III into Asia Minor. Roman legions cross the Hellespont and begin operations in Anatolia, extending the Syrian War into Seleucid heartland. This campaign marks the first time Roman armies have operated in Asia Minor on a large scale, establishing Rome's military presence in the region and foreshadowing Rome's eventual dominance over the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Roman pursuit of Antiochus III into Asia Minor is well-documented. The campaign began in 191 BCE and continued into 190 BCE.

events.title: Aetolian League's fortunes decline as Roman dominance increases
events.description: The Aetolian League, which had invited Antiochus III to Greece in hopes of countering Macedonian power, finds itself increasingly dependent on Rome following the Roman victory at Thermopylae. The League's political autonomy is gradually eroded as Rome establishes itself as the dominant power in Greece. This marks the beginning of the end of the Aetolian League's independence.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Aetolian decline is well-documented, but specific events in 191 BCE are not individually attested. This entry reflects the broader political context.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued stability under Emperor Wen
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continues his reign without major military conflicts or internal crises. The Han state remains stable and prosperous. This period of stability allows for continued cultural and technological advancement, including developments in literature, philosophy, and administration.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Wen's reign and general policies are well-documented. Specific events in 191 BCE are not individually attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise details of the Battle of Thermopylae in 191 BCE are debated. Some sources emphasize the role of a flanking maneuver, while others stress the superior Roman discipline and organization. The extent to which Antiochus III's defeat was due to tactical errors versus Roman superiority is contested. The significance of the Aetolian League's role in inviting Antiochus III is also debated; some scholars argue they were manipulated by Rome, while others suggest they made a calculated but failed strategic choice.
historiographic_note: For 191 BCE, sources are rich for the Syrian War and Roman-Seleucid conflict. Livy and Polybius provide detailed accounts, though both wrote centuries later. Chinese sources remain contemporary in spirit but compiled centuries after events. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political history; economic and social developments are underrepresented. The perspective is Greco-Roman-centric; other regions are largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Thermopylae led to pursuit into Asia Minor
Battle of Thermopylae led to eventual Treaty of Apamea
Aetolian decline accelerated after Syrian War
